rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A. VS. JOHNREY I MAGCALING ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Enforce Settlement (C.C.P. Section 664.6) Points And Authorities; And Delcaration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 5, 2025, line 1, PLAINTIFF BANK OF AMERICA, N.A.'S Motion To Enforce Settlement (C.C.P. Section 664.6) Plaintiff Bank of America N.A.'s unopposed motion to enforce settlement is granted. Plaintiff is directed to submit a proposed judgment for $10,188.11 against defendant Johnrey Magcaling; this is the settlement amount ($12,047.11) less what defendant has paid ($1,859.00). Plaintiff may seek additional costs through a cost bill after judgment. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: COURTNEY ELISE STONE VS. MARRIOT INTERNATIONAL, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 5, 2025, line 4, DEFENDANT FERGUSON ENTERPRISES, LLC ERRONEOUSLY SUED AS FERGUSON FACILITIES RENOVATION'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Defendant's (FERGUSON ENTERPRISES, LLC) motion to strike is denied. The court liberally construes the complaint per Code of Civil Procedure section 452. Plaintiff pleads sufficient facts showing malice, oppression, or fraud to support the claim for punitive damages. (Civil Code section 3294(a).) "[M]alice does not require actual intent to harm. [Citation.] Conscious disregard for the safety of another may be sufficient where the defendant is aware of the probable dangerous consequences of his or her conduct and he or she willfully fails to avoid such consequences." (Pfeifer v. John Crane, Inc. (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1270, 1299; see also College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 713 ["For plaintiffs attempting to prove malice by showing a 'conscious disregard' of their rights as opposed to an actual intent to harm, the Act imposed additional requirements of 'despicable' and 'willful' defense conduct."].) Here, plaintiff's allegations that defendant's officers, directors, and managing agents knew of the dangers of the shower door and continued to distribute it support the request for exemplary damages. (Complaint (CGC-24-613280), pars. 17-18, 26-30, 59-68.) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: MACARTHUR MCDANIEL II VS. CITY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR LEAVE TO Amend Plaintiff'S Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 5, 2025, line 5, PLAINTIFF MACARTHUR MCDANIEL'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO Amend Plaintiff'S Complaint (tenatative ruling part 1 of 2) Plaintiff's motion for leave to file a first amended complaint is denied. The court's reasoning is as follows. Plaintiff's proposed amendment does not seek to add causes of action but merely to plead facts and legal theories that support a contention that defendant was grossly negligent or reckless. The amendment to the complaint is unnecessary for plaintiff to make those arguments. "California does not recognize a distinct cause of action for 'gross negligence' independent of a statutory basis." (Saenz v. Whitewater Voyages, Inc. (1990) 226 Cal.App.3d 758, 766, fn. 9; see also Continental Ins. Co. v. American Protection Industries (1987) 197 Cal.App.3d 322, 328, 330 ["Do Our Courts Recognize a Cause of Action for 'Gross Negligence'? No...[I]n light of the adoption of the doctrine of comparative negligence in California, any attempt to categorize gross negligence separately from ordinary negligence is unnecessary."].) Instead, gross negligence is a species of negligence that is already put at issue by plaintiff's negligence cause of action. (See Hass v. RhodyCo Productions (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 11, 32 ["regardless of whether gross negligence can be a separate cause of action, and/or the Hass Family could have alleged gross negligence in the Complaint in anticipation of RhodyCo's likely defense, they were not required to do so"].) Because plaintiff did not plead around the release, the City is not required to refute the gross negligence theory in order to meet its initial burden of production on summary judgment. Plaintiff may rely on gross negligence to create a triable issue of fact in opposition to the summary judgment motion. (See Hass, supra, at p. 33.) Thus, there is no effect on plaintiff's claims or summary judgment position by the court's denial of the motion, nor would plaintiff be precluded at trial from presenting evidence of gross negligence. But the effect on the City would be to moot its summary judgment motion, and would preclude it from moving for summary judgment entirely in light of the approaching trial date of July 14, 2025. This order is without prejudice to a renewed motion to amend in the event the trial date is continued. Motions to continue trial are heard in Department 206 by the Presiding Judge (Local Rule 6.0(B)), and this court expresses no view on the merits of a motion to continue trial. (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Discovery Responses
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 5, 2025, line 8, PLAINTIFF STEPHEN WILLIAMS' Motion To Compel Discovery Responses The motion to compel discovery responses and for sanctions is granted in part and denied in part. The court exercises its discretion to consider defendant Vincent Vandell's late-filed opposition. If plaintiff Stephen Williams contests this tentative, the court will continue the matter for hearing to enable Williams to submit a reply. Based on the moving papers and opposition, the court's indicated order is to grant the motion and compel objection-free responses to the form interrogatories, special interrogatories, and requests for production, within 20 days of entry of this order. Vandell contends that post-judgment discovery is premature because he may yet appeal, but he offers no authority that the fact of a pending appeal window stays discovery. Indeed, even an appeal does not automatically stay enforcement of a money judgment. The court does not find substantial justification for the opposition to the discovery motion. The court's indicated order is to grant sanctions of $60 payable by Vandell to Williams within 20 days of entry of this order, representing Williams's filing fees incurred in bringing this motion. The court does not award attorneys' fees because Williams is self-represented and may not recover his ordinary fees as discovery sanctions. (See Argaman v. Ratan (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 1173, 1179.) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: SWISH ANALYTICS, INC. VS. ODDSJAM, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Discovery From Defendants Oddsjam, Inc. And Opticodds, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Monday, May 5, 2025, line 10, PLAINTIFF SWISH ANALYTICS, INC. A DELAWARE CORPORATION'S Motion To Compel Discovery From Defendants Oddsjam, Inc. And Opticodds, Inc. (tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) Plaintiff's motion to compel jurisdictional discovery is granted in part and continued to June 9, 2025 for further hearing. On April 18, 2025, after plaintiff filed this motion, defendant produced an additional 1,076 documents that purportedly respond to plaintiff's discovery requests. (Bloodworth Decl., par. 29.) In addition, defendant has represented that more documents will be provided once the parties agree on the terms of a protective order. (Id. at 30.) The parties are ordered to meet and confer forthwith on the terms of the protective order. In the meantime, the court orders the following: The court overrules defendants' objection to the lookback period identified by plaintiff. Defendants' production shall relate back to defendants' foundings: OpticOdds (September 2023) and OddsJam (April 2021). (Restivo Decl., par. 3.) The evidence is "likely to lead to the production of evidence of facts establishing jurisdiction" and a lookback period of "several years" prior to the filing of the complaint is reasonable. (Hardell v. Vanzyl (2024) 102 Cal.App.5th 960, 975, 973.) Defendants fail to show that such production is unduly burdensome. (W. Pico Furniture Co. of L.A. v. Super. Ct. (1961) 56 Cal.2d 407, 417.) The court overrules the relevance and overbreadth objections to RFP No. 1. While the relationship among the defendant, the forum, and litigation determines jurisdiction (not plaintiff's or third parties' contacts), intentional targeting can support jurisdiction and the requested information meets the discovery standard. (Walden v. Fiore (2014) 571 U.S. 277.) The court overrules the relevance and overbreadth objections to RFP No. 30. Documents related to defendants' efforts to sell, promote, or market Odds information to persons located in California are relevant to the jurisdictional inquiry. The court overrules defendants' objections to producing internal documents (RFPs 30, 36, 39, and 41). Internal documents regarding defendants' California contacts and its ambitions are relevant to intentional targeting and purposeful availment of the benefits of the forum state. (Photo, Inc. v. Brand Technologies., Inc. (9th Cir. 2011) 647 F.3d 1218, 1229.) For the responses to which defendants are claiming a privilege, defendants shall produce a privilege log. Responsive documents and privilege log shall be produced by May 19, 2025. Defendants shall provide information regarding custodians, non-custodial sources, and search terms by May 19, 2025. To the extent any issues remain that were raised by this motion, the parties may file supplemental briefs of no more than five pages by June 2, 2025, regarding any outstanding issues. (end of part 1, see part 2) = (301/CVA)
